edges.description: Unchecked complement activation generates C5a and membrane attack complex on endothelial cells.
VWF multimer release and subendothelial collagen exposure recruit and activate platelets on the damaged endothelial surface.
Fibrin-platelet thrombi occlude glomerular capillaries and arterioles.
Complement-driven microthrombi can also affect extrarenal vascular beds.
edges.target: Systemic TMA and Extrarenal Organ Involvement

edges.description: Platelet consumption in microthrombi produces thrombocytopenia.
Gastrointestinal TMA involvement can manifest with diarrhea.
edges.source: Systemic TMA and Extrarenal Organ Involvement

edges.description: Cerebral TMA can produce seizures as a neurologic extrarenal manifestation.
edges.source: Systemic TMA and Extrarenal Organ Involvement

edges.description: Cerebral vascular thrombosis can cause ischemic stroke.
edges.source: Systemic TMA and Extrarenal Organ Involvement

edges.description: Glomerular and arteriolar microthrombi cause acute renal ischemic injury.
Glomerular endothelial injury and capillary thrombosis disrupt filtration barrier function.
Renal microvascular ischemia contributes to severe hypertension.
Erythrocyte shearing through thrombosed microvasculature produces MAHA.
Mechanical shearing through microthrombi produces schistocytes.
Hemolysis and organ ischemia elevate serum LDH.

nodes.description: The kidney is the primary target organ in aHUS. Acute kidney injury results from thrombotic microangiopathy in glomerular capillaries and arterioles, with rapid rise in serum creatinine and oliguria.

evidence.reference_title: Genetics and outcome of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome: a nationwide French series comparing children and adults.
evidence.snippet: progression to ESRD after the first aHUS episode was more frequent in adults (46% versus 16%; P<0.001)

nodes.description: The central mechanism of aHUS is loss of regulation of the alternative complement pathway on endothelial cell surfaces. Normally, complement factor H (CFH), membrane cofactor protein (MCP/CD46), and factor I (CFI) cooperate to inactivate C3b deposited on host cell surfaces. In aHUS, genetic mutations in these regulators or gain-of-function mutations in complement effectors (CFB, C3) lead to unchecked C3 convertase (C3bBb) activity and amplification loop activation on endothelial surfaces.

evidence.reference_title: Recommendations for diagnosis and treatment of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (aHUS): an expert consensus statement from the Rare Diseases Committee of the Brazilian Society of Nephrology (COMDORA-SBN).
evidence.snippet: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS) is a rare cause of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) caused by the dysregulation of the alternative complement pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Atypical aHUS: State of the art.
evidence.snippet: Tremendous advances in our understanding of the thrombotic microangiopathies (TMAs) have revealed distinct disease mechanisms within this heterogeneous group of diseases.

nodes.description: Gastrointestinal involvement is common in aHUS, with diarrhea being among the most frequent extrarenal manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic predictors of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome phenotype and outcome.
evidence.snippet: extrarenal organ manifestations occur in 19%-38% of patients within six months of initial disease presentation (dependent on organ)

nodes.description: LDH is markedly elevated reflecting both hemolysis and tissue ischemia.

evidence.reference_title: Long-Term Efficacy and Safety of the Long-Acting Complement C5 Inhibitor Ravulizumab for the Treatment of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome in Adults.
evidence.snippet: Normalization of platelet count, serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), and hemoglobin observed in the 26-week initial evaluation period was sustained

nodes.description: Microscopic or gross hematuria from glomerular endothelial damage.

evidence.reference_title: Recessive mutations in DGKE cause atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals present with aHUS before age 1 year, have persistent hypertension, hematuria and proteinuria

nodes.description: Often severe and can be malignant, resulting from renal ischemia and activation of the renin-angiotensin system.

evidence.reference_title: Recessive mutations in DGKE cause atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals present with aHUS before age 1 year, have persistent hypertension, hematuria and proteinuria

nodes.description: Uncontrolled complement activation generates membrane attack complex (MAC/C5b-9) on endothelial cells, causing direct cell injury. Sublytic MAC deposition induces endothelial activation with upregulation of adhesion molecules, tissue factor expression, and loss of thrombomodulin. Injured endothelium releases von Willebrand factor (vWF) multimers and exposes subendothelial collagen.

evidence.reference_title: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The understanding of the role of the membrane attack complex in some TMAs has led to the introduction of pharmacologic inhibition of complement C5, which greatly improved prognosis.

evidence.reference_title: Long-Term Efficacy and Safety of the Long-Acting Complement C5 Inhibitor Ravulizumab for the Treatment of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome in Adults.
evidence.snippet: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS) is a rare, complex, multisystem disease of dysregulated complement activity, characterized by progressive thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA), acute kidney injury, and multiorgan dysfunction

nodes.description: Mechanical destruction of red blood cells passing through damaged and thrombosed microvasculature, evidenced by schistocytes on peripheral blood smear, elevated LDH, and reduced haptoglobin.

evidence.reference_title: Recommendations for diagnosis and treatment of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (aHUS).
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of TMA is made clinically by the triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and organ damage (mainly acute kidney injury).

nodes.description: VWF multimers released from activated endothelium and exposed subendothelial collagen promote platelet adhesion and aggregation. Tissue factor expression on activated endothelium triggers the coagulation cascade, generating fibrin-platelet microthrombi in arterioles and capillaries, particularly in the glomerular microvasculature.

evidence.reference_title: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: These all result in pathologic features of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA), which cause endothelial damage and organ injury.

nodes.description: Glomerular injury from TMA leads to proteinuria of variable degree.

evidence.reference_title: Recessive mutations in DGKE cause atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Affected individuals present with aHUS before age 1 year, have persistent hypertension, hematuria and proteinuria (sometimes in the nephrotic range)

nodes.description: Serum haptoglobin is reduced or undetectable as a marker of intravascular hemolysis in the setting of MAHA.

evidence.reference_title: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: TMAs manifest with a microangiopathic hemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and commonly acute kidney injury.

nodes.description: Fibrin-platelet thrombi occlude glomerular capillaries and arterioles, causing mechanical shearing of red blood cells (schistocyte formation), consumptive thrombocytopenia, and ischemic injury to renal parenchyma. Progressive thrombotic microangiopathy leads to acute kidney injury and, if untreated, irreversible renal damage with cortical necrosis and progression to end-stage renal disease.

evidence.reference_title: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: These all result in pathologic features of thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA), which cause endothelial damage and organ injury. TMAs manifest with a microangiopathic hemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and commonly acute kidney injury.

nodes.description: Fragmented red blood cells (schistocytes) on peripheral blood smear are a hallmark finding of thrombotic microangiopathy, resulting from mechanical shearing of erythrocytes through damaged microvasculature.

evidence.reference_title: Atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: TMAs manifest with a microangiopathic hemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and commonly acute kidney injury.

nodes.description: Neurological involvement can occur from cerebral TMA, manifesting as seizures, confusion, or encephalopathy. Extrarenal organ manifestations occur in 19-38% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic predictors of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome phenotype and outcome.
evidence.snippet: extrarenal organ manifestations occur in 19%-38% of patients within six months of initial disease presentation (dependent on organ)

nodes.description: Ischemic stroke from cerebral microvascular thrombosis, occurring in a subset of patients with extrarenal TMA manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic predictors of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome phenotype and outcome.
evidence.snippet: extrarenal organ manifestations occur in 19%-38% of patients within six months of initial disease presentation (dependent on organ)

nodes.description: Although kidney involvement dominates aHUS, thrombotic microangiopathy can affect extrarenal organ systems. Gastrointestinal and neurologic involvement can manifest as diarrhea, seizures, encephalopathy, or stroke.
nodes.id: Systemic TMA and Extrarenal Organ Involvement

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic predictors of atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome phenotype and outcome.
evidence.snippet: extrarenal organ manifestations occur in 19%-38% of patients within six months of initial disease presentation (dependent on organ)

nodes.description: Consumptive thrombocytopenia due to platelet consumption in microvascular thrombi. Platelet counts typically 20,000-150,000/uL.

evidence.reference_title: Recommendations for diagnosis and treatment of Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (aHUS).
evidence.snippet: The diagnosis of TMA is made clinically by the triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and organ damage (mainly acute kidney injury).

evidence.reference_title: Terminal complement inhibitor eculizumab in atypical hemolytic-uremic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Eculizumab resulted in increases in the platelet count; in trial 1, the mean increase in the count from baseline to week 26 was 73×10(9) per liter (P<0.001).
